---
title: "Looking into Nathan's Famous Hot Dog Eating Contest with Closeread"
author: Amara McGowan
format:
  closeread-html:
    cr-style:
      narrative-background-color-sidebar: darkgreen
      section-background-color: transparent
      narrative-text-color-overlay: black
      narrative-text-color-sidebar: white
---

```{r}
#| include: false
library(readr)
library(tidyverse)
```

```{r}
#| include: false
#hotdog_clean <- read_csv("Amara-McGowan/hotdog-data-storytelling-module/hotdog_clean.csv")
hotdog_clean <- read_csv("hotdog_clean.csv")

hotdog_men <- hotdog_clean |> filter(Sex == 1)
hotdog_women <- hotdog_clean |> filter(Sex == 0)

hotdog_men_min <- hotdog_men |> mutate(Hotdogs_per_minute = Dogs/Time)
hotdog_women_min <-hotdog_women |> mutate(Hotdogs_per_minute = Dogs/Time)

ggplot(data = hotdog_men_min, aes(x = Year, y = Hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Per Minute", title = "Amount of Hotdogs Consumed Per Minute by Winners")
```

:::{#cr-intro}

![Nathan's Webpage](https://assets-us-01.kc-usercontent.com/7a9cec6a-38b8-00ad-2f1c-269f0461d7dc/a65fb3e8-370a-4f06-852c-39610ca5fad7/frankie.png) 

# Welcome video

If you're interested in understanding how competitive eaters are able to consume so much food, here is a video referencing Nathan's Famous Hot Dog Eating Contest and the science behind it. 

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/D9DZmoRY0H4?si=AVxTT6zn5CAQxNfR" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe> 

# Introduction

Nathan's Famous Hot Dog Eating Contest is a competitive eating competition where contestants are given 10 minutes to eat as many hotdogs (bun and all) as they can manage. In its early years the duration of the competition varied, but for the past decade it has been a 10-minute cutoff.

# TODO: data storytelling intro (let ppl know what they're getting into)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Learning Objectives" appearance="minimal"}
By the end of this tutorial, students will be able to:

1. Create a "scrollytelling" document with Closeread.

2. Create scatterplots to look at patterns in data.

3. Form questions about the data based on visualizations. 

4. Investigate questions about the data.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Activity Length" appearance="minimal"}
This could be suitable of an out of class activity or one that spans one
or more class periods.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Methods" appearance="minimal"}
**Technology Requirement:**

This activity is designed to be completed primarily in `R` using a
Quarto document workflow. Students should be comfortable importing data
and making basic plots with packages from the tidyverse
(especially `readr` and `ggplot2`).
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true" title="Research Questions" appearance="minimal"}

During this lab, you'll investigate the following research questions:

+ Question one
+ Question two

:::


### Data

The `hotdog_clean.csv` dataset contains 69 rows, one per
Nathan's Famous Hot Dog Eating Contest Winner, with returning winners
showing up in the data multiple times. The primary variables in the 
data are `Dogs` and `Time`.

Download Data:
<!--TODO: [add later](add later) -->

<details>

<summary><b>Variable Descriptions</b></summary>

| Variable | Description |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Year | Year the Competition took place |
| Sex | Sex of the Winner (0 = Woman, 1 = Man) |
| Dogs | How many hotdogs the winner consumed |
| Winner | First and Last Name of the Winner |
| Time | Duration of the competition in minutes |

</details>

#### Data Sources

-   [TODO: Wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan's_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest#By_year_(color-coded_by_belt_color)){target="_blank"}

::: @cr-intro

::::: {.cr-section}

:::{#cr-tibble1 .cr-sticky}
```{r}
#| warning: false
head(hotdog_clean)
```
:::

This module will help you learn how to use the quarto extension `closeread` to tell stories with your data. To the right is a tibble of the data we will be using. When you scroll, you'll see the tibble stays to the right and new text emerges. @cr-tibble1

This scrolling feature helps you tell stories about your data, which people have named "scrollytelling." @cr-tibble1
:::

::::: {#cr-main}

# Let's get started

To begin, you will need to set up Closeread. Copy and paste the following block of code into a new qmd file.

```markdown
---
title: My First Closeread
author: Your Name
format: closeread-html
---

Get the packages we need to work with our data (uncomment this chunk)
#```{r}
library(readr) # This will help us read in our data
library(tidyverse) # This will help us work with our data and make plots
#```

Get our data (uncomment this chunk)
#```{r}
hotdog_clean <- read_csv("hotdog_clean.csv")
#```

:::{#cr-intro}

This is where you will start working! The three colon symbols denote a section, which is how your closeread will be organized.

:::
```

Next, you will need to install the Closeread extension. At the bottom of your screen, you should have a "console" open where the output of your code appears as you run it in RStudio. Next to console, you'll see something called the "Terminal", open that up and paste in the following code:

```markdown
quarto add qmd-lab/closeread
```

Now, separate the data into `hotdog_men` a dataset of only the male winners, and `hotdog_women` a dataset of only the women who have won. 

::: {.callout-caution collapse = "true" title = "Reveal the Code" appearance = "minimal"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
hotdog_men <- hotdog_clean |> filter(Sex == 1)
hotdog_women <- hotdog_clean |> filter(Sex == 0)
```
:::


::::: @cr-main
